content.text: AWS offers purpose-built database services for every data model — relational, key-value, document, graph, in-memory, time series, and columnar analytics. The right choice depends on your data model, query patterns, consistency requirements, and scale. Understanding the trade-offs between these purpose-built services is one of the most valuable AWS architecture skills.

attrs.alt: AWS database service landscape showing RDS, Aurora, DynamoDB, ElastiCache, Redshift, DocumentDB, Neptune, and Timestream, with a decision guide and data model spectrum

content.text: RDS (Relational Database Service) provides managed deployments of standard relational database engines: MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server, and Db2. AWS handles hardware provisioning, OS patching, database software updates, automated backups, and Multi-AZ failover. You retain control over schema, queries, parameters, and instance sizing.

— a synchronous standby replica in a different AZ is maintained at all times. On primary failure, RDS automatically fails over to the standby (typically 60–120 seconds). The endpoint DNS name is updated; applications reconnect to the new primary with no code change.

— asynchronous replicas that serve read traffic, offloading the primary. Can be created in the same region or cross-region (for DR). Promote a read replica to a standalone DB instance in minutes if the primary fails.

— a fully managed connection pool that sits between your application and RDS. Reduces connection overhead for Lambda or containerized workloads that open many short-lived connections; improves failover time from 60s to under 30s.
— daily snapshot + transaction logs, retained for 1–35 days. Point-in-time recovery to any second within the retention window. Snapshots can be copied cross-region manually for additional DR.

resource "aws_db_instance" "postgres" {
  identifier        = "prod-postgres"
  engine            = "postgres"
  engine_version    = "16.2"
  instance_class    = "db.t3.medium"
  allocated_storage = 100
  storage_type      = "gp3"
  storage_encrypted = true

  db_name  = "appdb"
  username = "dbadmin"
  password = var.db_password

  multi_az               = true
  db_subnet_group_name   = aws_db_subnet_group.main.name
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.rds.id]

  backup_retention_period = 14
  deletion_protection     = true
  skip_final_snapshot     = false
  final_snapshot_identifier = "prod-postgres-final"
}

Aurora is AWS's own relational database engine, wire-compatible with MySQL and PostgreSQL. It achieves up to 5× the throughput of MySQL and 3× PostgreSQL on the same hardware by decoupling storage from compute and distributing the storage layer across 3 AZs with 6 copies of data.
— data is written across 6 storage nodes (2 per AZ) automatically. Storage grows in 10 GB increments up to 128 TB. There is no 'volume size' to configure up front; you pay for what you use.
— one writer instance and up to 15 reader instances all share the same storage layer. Readers are immediately consistent; the replication lag is typically under 10 ms.

— the compute layer scales from 0.5 ACUs to 128 ACUs (Aurora Capacity Units) in fine-grained increments within seconds. Ideal for unpredictable or bursty workloads, or for development databases that should scale to near-zero when idle.

— replicates the entire Aurora cluster to up to 5 secondary regions with typical lag under 1 second. Secondary regions can serve reads; in a disaster, a secondary can be promoted to primary in under 1 minute.

resource "aws_rds_cluster" "aurora_pg" {
  cluster_identifier      = "aurora-prod"
  engine                  = "aurora-postgresql"
  engine_version          = "16.2"
  database_name           = "appdb"
  master_username         = "dbadmin"
  master_password         = var.db_password
  backup_retention_period = 14
  storage_encrypted       = true
  deletion_protection     = true
  vpc_security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.rds.id]
  db_subnet_group_name    = aws_db_subnet_group.main.name
}

# Writer instance
resource "aws_rds_cluster_instance" "writer" {
  identifier         = "aurora-prod-writer"
  cluster_identifier = aws_rds_cluster.aurora_pg.id
  instance_class     = "db.r6g.large"
  engine             = aws_rds_cluster.aurora_pg.engine
  engine_version     = aws_rds_cluster.aurora_pg.engine_version
}

DynamoDB is a fully managed key-value and document database that delivers single-digit millisecond performance at any scale. There are no servers to manage; capacity is measured in read capacity units (RCUs) and write capacity units (WCUs), and DynamoDB handles all partitioning, replication, and storage internally.

— every item has a partition key (and optional sort key). All items with the same partition key live on the same partition. The primary access pattern must be designed into the key schema upfront — DynamoDB is optimized for known, predictable queries, not ad-hoc SQL joins.
— On-Demand (pay per request, no planning) and Provisioned (set RCU/WCU with optional Auto Scaling). Use On-Demand for variable traffic; Provisioned + Auto Scaling for predictable load at lower cost.
— multi-active replication across up to 4 regions. Each region accepts writes; DynamoDB resolves conflicts using last-writer-wins (per item). Typical replication lag is under 1 second.
— a time-ordered stream of item-level changes (insert, update, delete). Used to trigger Lambda functions, replicate to Elasticsearch, or power event-driven architectures.
— DynamoDB Accelerator, an in-memory cache that sits transparently in front of DynamoDB. Reduces latency from single-digit milliseconds to microseconds for read-heavy workloads.

import boto3

ddb = boto3.resource('dynamodb')
table = ddb.Table('users')

# Write an item
table.put_item(Item={
    'userId': 'u-123',           # partition key
    'createdAt': '2024-01-15',   # sort key
    'name': 'Alice',
    'email': 'alice@example.com',
    'plan': 'pro'
})

# Read by primary key (O(1) lookup)
response = table.get_item(Key={'userId': 'u-123', 'createdAt': '2024-01-15'})
print(response['Item']['name'])   # Alice

# Conditional write (optimistic locking)
table.update_item(
    Key={'userId': 'u-123', 'createdAt': '2024-01-15'},
    UpdateExpression='SET #p = :newplan',
    ConditionExpression='#p = :old',
    ExpressionAttributeNames={'#p': 'plan'},
    ExpressionAttributeValues={':newplan': 'enterprise', ':old': 'pro'}
)

ElastiCache provides managed Redis and Memcached clusters. In-memory stores hold data in RAM, enabling sub-millisecond read latency — roughly 100× faster than reading from RDS. The primary use case is caching: store the results of expensive database queries or API calls in ElastiCache and serve subsequent requests from the cache.

— Redis supports rich data structures (sorted sets, lists, hashes, streams), persistence (AOF and RDB snapshots), replication, Pub/Sub, and Lua scripting. Memcached is simpler and supports multi-threaded operation; use it only if you need pure horizontal scaling of a simple cache with no data structure requirements.

— shards data across up to 500 nodes, each with a primary and up to 5 replicas. Enables horizontal scaling beyond a single node's memory capacity. Keyspace is partitioned into 16,384 hash slots across shards.

— database query cache (cache-aside: check Redis first, then DB on miss), session store (HTTP session data across stateless app servers), rate limiting (atomic INCR on a key with TTL), leaderboards (sorted sets with ZADD/ZRANGE), pub/sub messaging.
— a newer option that scales Redis capacity automatically without managing shards or replication; you pay per ECU (ElastiCache Compute Unit) and GB of data stored.

import redis, json, boto3

# Connect to ElastiCache Redis cluster endpoint
r = redis.Redis(host='my-cluster.cache.amazonaws.com', port=6379, ssl=True)

def get_user(user_id: str) -> dict:
    cache_key = f'user:{user_id}'
    cached = r.get(cache_key)
    if cached:
        return json.loads(cached)           # Cache hit

    # Cache miss — fetch from RDS
    ddb = boto3.resource('dynamodb')
    item = ddb.Table('users').get_item(Key={'userId': user_id})['Item']

    r.setex(cache_key, 300, json.dumps(item))  # Cache for 5 min
    return item

Redshift is AWS's columnar data warehouse, designed for OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) — complex queries that aggregate millions or billions of rows. Unlike OLTP databases (RDS, DynamoDB) optimized for many small transactions, Redshift is optimized for a smaller number of long-running analytical queries.
— stores data column-by-column rather than row-by-row. A query selecting 3 columns from a 100-column table reads only 3% of the data. Compression ratios are also much higher for columnar data.
— queries are distributed across multiple nodes (compute nodes). Each node processes its slice of data in parallel; the leader node merges results.
— query data directly in S3 (Parquet, ORC, CSV) without loading it into Redshift. Useful for infrequently queried historical data or a data lake architecture.
— no cluster management; capacity scales automatically. Pay per RPU-second (Redshift Processing Unit). Ideal for intermittent analytics workloads.
MongoDB-compatible workloads migrating to AWS; user profiles, product catalogs, CMS content
Social networks (who-follows-whom), fraud detection (connected entity patterns), knowledge graphs, recommendation engines
IoT sensor readings, DevOps metrics, application telemetry — trillions of events per day queried by time range
Apache Cassandra-compatible workloads; write-heavy, wide-table data at scale
Immutable, cryptographically verifiable transaction logs; finance, supply chain, regulatory audit trails
Redis-compatible with Multi-AZ durability (transaction log); primary DB use cases, not just cache
RDS vs Aurora vs DynamoDB: Choosing Between the Three Core Services

These three services cover the vast majority of database workloads on AWS. The key differentiator is whether your data model requires SQL joins, how much you need to control capacity, and your scale requirements.

when: you have an existing application using a specific engine (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server), your team is most familiar with that engine, or you need maximum control over parameters and version selection. Standard choice for lift-and-shift migrations.

when: you are starting a new relational workload, want higher performance and built-in HA, or need Serverless v2 for variable load. For any new MySQL or PostgreSQL deployment on AWS, Aurora is generally the preferred choice over standard RDS.

when: you have known, predictable access patterns (not arbitrary queries), need to scale to millions of requests per second, or require global multi-region writes. DynamoDB shines for user sessions, game state, IoT device state, shopping carts, and leaderboards.
— RDS, Aurora, and ElastiCache should be in private subnets with no public IP. Application servers in public or private subnets connect over the VPC network; no internet path to the database.
— restrict inbound to the database port (5432 for PostgreSQL, 3306 for MySQL, 6379 for Redis) only from the application security group. Deny all other sources, including office IP ranges.
— RDS and Aurora support IAM database authentication: an EC2 instance or Lambda generates a short-lived token from its IAM role instead of a static password. Eliminates password rotation burden.

— store database credentials in AWS Secrets Manager rather than environment variables or config files. Secrets Manager supports automatic rotation for RDS and Aurora credentials via a built-in Lambda rotation function.

— enable KMS encryption on all RDS, Aurora, and DynamoDB resources at creation. Encryption cannot be added to an existing unencrypted RDS instance in-place; you must restore a snapshot with encryption enabled.

Unlike relational databases where each entity type gets its own table, DynamoDB best practice is to store all entity types in a single table. This eliminates costly joins and keeps all access patterns within the same partition key space. The pattern uses generic attribute names (PK, SK) and overloads them with entity-specific values.

Single-table design requires thinking through access patterns before creating the table. Enumerate every query your application needs, then design PK/SK combinations that satisfy all of them. Global Secondary Indexes (GSIs) add alternate access patterns without changing the base table's key schema.

Aurora Serverless v2 scales the database compute in fine-grained increments (0.5 ACU steps) within seconds — far faster than traditional Auto Scaling. This makes it ideal for multi-tenant SaaS applications, development databases, and workloads with large traffic spikes.
— set to 0.5 ACU for development (scales to near-zero when idle). For production, set a minimum that handles baseline traffic without cold start latency.
— set a ceiling to prevent runaway costs from unexpected traffic spikes. 1 ACU ≈ 2 GB RAM. A db.r6g.xlarge equivalent is 4 ACUs.
— an Aurora cluster can have one provisioned writer and multiple Serverless v2 readers. This provides a stable write path with elastic read capacity.

— $0.12/ACU-hour (us-east-1). A cluster running at 2 ACUs continuously costs $0.24/hour — slightly more than a db.t3.small at $0.034/hour, so Serverless v2 is only cost-effective when the average utilization is significantly below the provisioned minimum.

Both RDS and Aurora support read replicas for scaling read-heavy workloads. The key difference is replication architecture: RDS replicas use asynchronous binary log replication (lag can reach seconds), while Aurora replicas share the same storage layer (lag typically under 10 ms).

Application code typically uses two connection strings: a writer endpoint (primary) for INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE, and a reader endpoint (load-balanced across replicas) for SELECT queries. RDS Proxy can manage both endpoint pools for Lambda and container workloads.
— loading a list of 100 users and then issuing a separate query for each user's orders produces 101 database round-trips. Use JOIN or batch fetching. With DynamoDB, use
for up to 100 keys per call.

— Lambda can spin up thousands of concurrent instances, each opening a DB connection. RDS has a max_connections limit (roughly 4,000 for db.r6g.4xlarge). RDS Proxy pools connections and queues excess requests instead of rejecting them.

— a partition key chosen poorly (e.g., a sequential ID or a status field with only 3 values) concentrates all traffic on a single partition. Design partition keys for even distribution: user ID, device ID, or a random suffix.
option that prevents accidental Terraform or console deletion. Enable it on all production databases. A
on a production database without this flag is irreversible.

— DynamoDB On-Demand mode costs roughly 6-7× more per request than Provisioned + Auto Scaling at consistent high traffic. Switch to Provisioned once your traffic is predictable enough to set WCU/RCU targets.
AWS provides layered observability for database services. Understanding which metrics signal problems — before users notice — separates reactive from proactive operations.

— visualizes which SQL statements and wait events consume the most database time. Identifies slow queries and lock contention without enabling slow query log manually. Free for 7-day retention; paid tier extends to 2 years.
— 50+ OS-level metrics (CPU steal, disk I/O per process, network) at up to 1-second granularity. Published to CloudWatch Logs rather than standard CloudWatch metrics.

# CloudWatch alarm: RDS free storage below 10 GB
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "rds_low_storage" {
  alarm_name          = "rds-prod-low-storage"
  comparison_operator = "LessThanThreshold"
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  metric_name         = "FreeStorageSpace"
  namespace           = "AWS/RDS"
  period              = 300
  statistic           = "Average"
  threshold           = 10737418240   # 10 GB in bytes
  dimensions = {
    DBInstanceIdentifier = aws_db_instance.postgres.id
  }
  alarm_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}

# Alarm: DynamoDB throttled requests
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "ddb_throttle" {
  alarm_name          = "ddb-users-table-throttled"
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanThreshold"
  evaluation_periods  = 1
  metric_name         = "ThrottledRequests"
  namespace           = "AWS/DynamoDB"
  period              = 60
  statistic           = "Sum"
  threshold           = 0
  dimensions = {
    TableName = "users"
  }
  alarm_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}

AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) migrates databases to AWS with minimal downtime. DMS supports homogeneous migrations (Oracle to Oracle on RDS) and heterogeneous migrations (Oracle to Aurora PostgreSQL, SQL Server to MySQL).

— DMS first copies the full existing data (full-load phase), then uses Change Data Capture to continuously replicate ongoing changes. The cutover window is minimal — stop the source, let CDC drain, switch the application connection string.

— for heterogeneous migrations, SCT converts DDL (table definitions, stored procedures, functions) from the source engine's dialect to the target. It flags constructs that require manual rewriting (complex stored procedures, vendor-specific extensions).
— DMS can run as a continuous replication feed after the initial migration, keeping two databases in sync. Useful for gradual cutovers where you want to validate the target before switching traffic.

AWS provides purpose-built databases for every model: RDS/Aurora (relational SQL), DynamoDB (NoSQL key-value), ElastiCache (in-memory), Redshift (analytics), DocumentDB (documents), Neptune (graph), Timestream (time series).

Aurora is generally preferred over standard RDS for new MySQL/PostgreSQL workloads — it delivers higher performance, better HA, and Aurora Serverless v2 for variable load, at 20% higher base cost than RDS.
DynamoDB scales to unlimited throughput and storage, but requires designing access patterns into the key schema upfront — ad-hoc SQL queries are not its strength.
ElastiCache (Redis) adds a sub-millisecond caching layer in front of any database, dramatically reducing latency and cost for read-heavy workloads.
All database services should be placed in private subnets, encrypted with KMS, and accessed via IAM authentication or Secrets Manager — never with hardcoded credentials.
